did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses a customised DenseNet-121 deep learning model (a Densely Connected Convolutional Network) for melanoma detection and classification in dermatological images. The study evaluates the model's performance on white and dark skin images using metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, achieving approximately 98.5% accuracy for white skin and 99% for dark skin.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a customised DenseNet-121 model for melanoma detection. It includes specific sections on methodology (dataset, preprocessing, model design, training parameters), results (accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, AUC metrics for white and dark skin images), and a confusion matrix. Although published in conference proceedings (ICICI-2025), it reports new empirical findings rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Melanoma Detection using Customised DenseNet-121 for Dermatological image analysis
journal: Proceedings of the 3rd International Conference on Inventive Computing and Informatics (ICICI-2025)
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of early melanoma detection by proposing a customised DenseNet-121 deep learning model to overcome the limitations of traditional, subjective dermatological exams. The researchers utilized a modified version of the HAM10000 dataset, specifically separating 10% of the data as white skin images and deriving 100 dark skin images via augmentation to evaluate performance across different skin tones. The model architecture involved detaching fully connected layers from the pre-trained DenseNet-121 and attaching a custom classification head with global average pooling, a dense layer with ReLU activation, and dropout to prevent overfitting. Evaluation metrics including accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score were computed on both white and dark skin subsets, with the model achieving approximately 98.5% accuracy for white skin and 99% accuracy for dark skin images. The results demonstrate that the customised DenseNet-121 provides high sensitivity and low false positive rates, confirming its reliability for melanoma classification across diverse pigmentation levels. The authors conclude that this approach offers a robust tool for assisting healthcare professionals in accurate and timely melanoma diagnosis.

lesion_types_studied.details: The primary target of the binary classification task. The study uses a modified HAM10000 dataset to differentiate between melanoma and non-melanoma cases. The model achieves high accuracy (98.5% for white skin, 99% for dark skin) in detecting melanoma.

The negative class in the binary classification task. The dataset includes 'benign skin ailments' from the HAM10000 dataset, which are grouped as 'Non-Melanoma' or 'NotMelanoma' for the classification task.

all_task_types.details: The model performs binary classification to distinguish between melanoma and non-melanoma (benign) skin lesions. The paper explicitly states that images are divided into 'melanoma and non-melanoma categories using a softmax layer' and evaluates performance using classification metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC for both white and dark skin subsets.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2018
datasets_used.description: A public dataset of dermoscopy images depicting skin disorders, including melanoma and benign skin ailments, originally hosted on Harvard Dataverse and presented by Tschandl et al. The study uses an adapted version derived from this dataset.

use.training: Used for training the customised DenseNet-121 model. The dataset was split 90:10 for train-test, with 10% of the images separated as white skin images.
use.testing: Used for testing the model on white skin images (derived from the 10% split) and dark skin images (derived via augmentation).
use.other: The study utilized a Kaggle-derived version of HAM10000 (presented by Alexander Scarlat) which included data augmentation techniques.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To calculate and report True Positives, True Negatives, False Positives, and False Negatives for evaluating classification performance on white and dark skin subsets.
To assess the model's ability to discriminate between melanoma and non-melanoma classes by plotting True Positive Rate (TPR) against False Positive Rate (FPR) at various thresholds.
To report average values of performance metrics (Accuracy, Precision, Recall, F1-score) across white and dark skin image subsets.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics such as accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and ROC-AUC.

HAM10000 (White Skin Subset).notes: Confusion matrix values: 523 True Positives, 530 True Negatives, 4 False Positives, 12 False Negatives. The paper also reports slightly more precise values in Table 4.1: Accuracy 0.985, Precision 0.985, Recall 0.985, F1 0.985. Textual description mentions 98.503%, 98.513%, 98.503%, 98.503% for the respective metrics.

HAM10000 (Dark Skin Subset).notes: Confusion matrix values: 50 True Positives, 49 True Negatives, 1 False Positive, 0 False Negatives. The paper also reports slightly more precise values in Table 4.1: Accuracy 0.99, Precision 0.99, Recall 0.99, F1 0.989. Textual description mentions 99.00%, 99.01%, 99.00%, and 98.99% for the respective metrics.

architecture_details.dense_block_structure: Four dense blocks with transition layers; growth rate k=32; 1x1 bottleneck convolutions; 2x2 average pooling for downsampling
architecture_details.feature_extractor: Convolutional layers with frozen weights to extract spatial and texture-based features
classification_head: Fully Connected (Dense) layer with 128 neurons and ReLU activation

preprocessing_techniques: Resizing: All photos were downsized to a standard size of 224 x 224 using interpolation-based resize methods.
Normalization: Pixel values were normalized by dividing each value by 255, scaling them between 0 and 1.
Data Augmentation: The dataset was derived using data augmentation techniques to address imbalance and create dark skin subsets.
Shuffling: The dataset was randomly shuffled to eliminate inherent biases and ensure diversified learning.
One-hot Encoding: Class labels were translated into a structured format using one-hot encoding for binary classification compatibility.
Conversion to NumPy Arrays: Images were converted into NumPy arrays (X) and class labels (Y) for efficient storage and computation.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: The study utilized a modified version of the HAM10000 dataset from Kaggle (presented by Alexander Scarlat) which was derived using data augmentation techniques to address class imbalance.
100 dark skin images were derived separately from the dataset using data augmentation techniques to evaluate the model's performance on dark skin tones.

conclusions: The customised DenseNet-121 model effectively overcomes the limitations of traditional, subjective, and time-consuming dermatological exams for melanoma detection.
The proposed method achieves high classification accuracy across different skin tones, with approximately 98.5% accuracy for white skin images and 99% accuracy for dark skin images.
The model demonstrates strong sensitivity and low false positive rates, confirming its reliability for melanoma classification across diverse pigmentation levels.

The customised architecture, which detaches fully connected layers and attaches a custom classification head with global average pooling, dense layers, and dropout, successfully enhances feature reuse and reduces duplication compared to traditional CNN designs.
The results indicate that the model can assist doctors and healthcare professionals in identifying melanoma more accurately, leading to better patient outcomes through early diagnosis.
The study acknowledges limitations regarding the dataset size and diversity, suggesting that future research should focus on using diverse datasets to enhance accuracy and reliability.

Future improvements include combining lesion segmentation with attention-based mechanisms, enhancing precision via advanced data augmentation, fine-tuning hyperparameters, incorporating regularization techniques like Batch Normalization, and integrating explainable AI methods like GradCAM.
important_remarks: The study relies on a modified HAM10000 dataset where the 'dark skin' subset consists of only 100 images derived via augmentation, raising concerns about the generalizability and statistical power of the reported 99% accuracy for this demographic.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the difference in performance between white and dark skin subsets.

The dataset split methodology is ambiguous; the text states a 90:10 split with 10% separated as white skin images, but the specific allocation of the remaining 90% for training versus the dark skin test set is not clearly defined, increasing the risk of data leakage or non-independent test sets.

The model was trained and evaluated on a single, publicly available dataset (HAM10000 via Kaggle) without external validation on independent clinical cohorts, limiting the assessment of real-world clinical robustness.

The paper acknowledges limitations regarding dataset size and diversity, noting that future work requires diverse datasets to enhance reliability, which suggests current results may be overfitted to the specific distribution of the augmented HAM10000 subset.

The reported metrics (e.g., 99% accuracy for dark skin) are exceptionally high for melanoma detection tasks, which may indicate optimistic bias due to the small sample size (n=100) and synthetic nature of the dark skin images rather than true clinical efficacy.
